findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/recovery plan for the Jira comment cleanup: at minimum a dry-run mode that lists comments to be deleted, a pre-deletion export of comment IDs + content, and a documented abort/restore procedure if the cleanup partially fails.
evidence: 'the 13 capped tickets are de-duplicated so new comments can sync again (executed AFTER P1 is deployed to main)' — no dry-run, no backup, no rollback path mentioned
Jira comment deletion is irreversible via the API; a partial failure mid-cleanup would leave tickets in an inconsistent, potentially unrecoverable state
Criterion: 'Any destructive/irreversible step is an explicit, justified choice' AND 'The remedy is an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract'
findings.finding: The Jira comment de-duplication cleanup is irreversible (deleted comments cannot be restored from Jira) but the plan provides no rollback mechanism — no dry-run step, no pre-deletion backup of comment IDs/content, and no recovery procedure if the cleanup partially fails or deletes the wrong comments.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Jira data cleanup: the 13 capped tickets are de-duplicated'
scenarios: Cleanup script deletes a superset of duplicate comments (off-by-one in dedup logic), permanently removing canonical comments
Cleanup runs on wrong ticket range due to a filter bug, partially completing before failure — no way to identify what was deleted
findings.suggested_fix: Before deletion, export full comment payload (id, body, author, created) for all 13 tickets to a versioned artifact (e.g., a JSON file committed to the repo or stored in CI). Add a `--dry-run` flag to the cleanup script. Document: 'if cleanup fails mid-run, re-run with --dry-run to identify remaining duplicates; deleted comments are unrecoverable — contact Jira admin if canonical comments were removed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Create the four missing child tickets (P2 inbound-link direction fix, Observability/exit-code fix, Test-hardening, Jira data cleanup) so all five AC-named children are recorded in the store and the decomposition matches the plan's stated structure.
evidence: AC: 'All child tickets (P1, P2, observability, test-hardening, Jira-cleanup) are closed.'
Decomposition state: only florid-vigorous-urial (P1) is recorded; P2, observability, test-hardening, and Jira-cleanup have no child tickets.

Each of P2 (stops ~815 cycle-rejections/pass), Observability (changes exit semantics), Test-hardening (cross-cutting coverage), and Jira-cleanup (operational data remediation) delivers independent, testable value on its own.
Jira-cleanup is explicitly order-gated on P1 deploy, confirming it is a separate, sequenced unit of work.
findings.finding: The plan's own AC explicitly calls for 5 child tickets (P1, P2, observability, test-hardening, Jira-cleanup), but the decomposition store shows only 1 child recorded. P2 (inbound link direction), Observability (exit-code/logging), Test-hardening, and Jira-cleanup are each independently valuable and releasable outcomes with no corresponding child ticket, leaving ~80% of the planned decomposition unexecuted.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'All child tickets (P1, P2, observability, test-hardening, Jira-cleanup) are closed.'
scenarios: P2 fix is never tracked as a child ticket and falls through the cracks while the parent shows 'has children'.
Observability and test-hardening work is done ad-hoc inside P1's child rather than as a tracked deliverable, making it impossible to verify AC coverage independently.
Jira cleanup (data remediation of 13 capped tickets) has no ticket to gate on P1 deployment, risking premature or missed execution.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one child ticket per remaining concern: (a) P2 — swap inward/outward branches in `_diff_links_inbound`; (b) Observability — enable `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` / non-zero exit on silent sub-op failure; (c) Test-hardening — unit + integration + E2E for all three failure modes; (d) Jira-cleanup — dedup script for 13 capped tickets, gated on P1 merge to main.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a mandatory dry-run mode and a pre-deletion comment export (e.g., JSON snapshot per ticket) to the cleanup script so deletions can be audited and any accidental loss can be diagnosed; document that no rollback path exists once deletions execute.
evidence: Plan text contains no mention of a dry-run flag, comment export/backup, or any back-out procedure before executing deletions.
Jira comment DELETE is permanent; there is no recycle-bin or undo endpoint in the Jira REST API.
If the dedup script's 'canonical comment' selection logic is wrong (e.g., keeps the duplicate and deletes the original), real ticket history is permanently destroyed.
findings.finding: Comment deletion in Jira is irreversible via the API; the plan specifies no dry-run mode, no pre-deletion backup/export, and no rollback path — constituting a DATA LOSS RISK if the dedup logic misidentifies canonical vs. duplicate comments.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Jira data cleanup: the 13 capped tickets are de-duplicated'
scenarios: Dedup script misidentifies the 'original' comment for a ticket where timestamps are identical across duplicates; deletes the wrong copy, permanently destroying comment content.
Cleanup script is run against the wrong Jira project/instance; all deletions are irreversible.
findings.suggested_fix: Implement a two-phase cleanup: (1) `--dry-run` that prints what would be deleted with content preview, (2) export all comments to a timestamped JSON file before any deletions, (3) require explicit `--confirm` flag for destructive execution. Annotate the AC with 'DATA LOSS RISK: no rollback after phase 2'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Create and link a sibling ticket for Jira data cleanup (de-duplicate the 13 capped tickets) and add it to the child roster so the parent's cleanup criterion is covered.
evidence: Parent SC: 'Jira data cleanup: the 13 capped tickets are de-duplicated so new comments can sync again (executed AFTER P1 is deployed to main).'
Child Notes: 'The 13 capped Jira issues need a separate data cleanup (sibling ticket) AFTER this fix deploys, else the buggy reconciler re-inflates them.'
Complete sibling roster contains only: '1f3d-cb66-d3fc-4356' — no cleanup sibling exists.
container pairing: parent + 1f3d-cb66-d3fc-4356
findings.finding: The parent's 'Jira data cleanup' success criterion (de-duplicate the 13 capped tickets, executed AFTER P1 deploys) is not covered by any sibling in the provided roster. The child's Notes acknowledge this as a 'separate data cleanup (sibling ticket)' but no such sibling exists in the complete sibling roster.
findings.location: child 1f3d-cb66-d3fc-4356
scenarios: P1 fix is deployed; the 13 tickets remain at the 5000-comment cap; the reconciler cannot add new comments to them; the parent acceptance criterion '0 comment-cap failures' cannot be satisfied without the cleanup.
findings.suggested_fix: Open a sibling ticket scoped to: enumerate the 13 affected Jira keys, delete duplicate comments (preserving unique ones), and verify that comment count drops below 5000 so the reconciler can resume syncing. Link it as a dependency of the parent and gate its execution on P1 being live on main.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Create sibling tickets for P2 (_diff_links_inbound inward/outward swap fix), Observability (RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP enablement + non-zero exit), and a combined Test-hardening ticket covering all three failure modes with E2E coverage.
evidence: Parent SC: 'P2: an inbound Jira Blocks link round-trips to the correct rebar relation.'
Parent SC: 'Observability: a pass with any silent sub-op no-op reports failure (non-zero exit).'
Parent SC: 'Test hardening: unit + integration + E2E coverage added for all three failure modes AND same-kind gaps.'
Complete sibling roster: only '1f3d-cb66-d3fc-4356' — no P2, no observability, no combined test-hardening sibling.
container pairing: parent + 1f3d-cb66-d3fc-4356
findings.finding: The parent's P2 (inbound link direction fix), Observability, and 'Test hardening for all three failure modes' success criteria are not covered by this child or any sibling in the complete roster (only one child exists). These are owned-by-none gaps.
findings.location: child 1f3d-cb66-d3fc-4356
scenarios: P2 inbound link inversion remains unfixed — ~815 cycle-rejected link applies/pass continue.
Silent failure mode remains — passes with failed sub-ops still report 'converged' and exit 0.
No E2E tests covering all three failure modes exist.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `get_comment_map()` truncates per-issue inline comments for high-volume tickets (Jira Cloud caps the `comment` field in search responses); if so, extend P1 to either (a) detect truncation via `comment.total > len(comment.comments)` and fall back to per-ticket `get_comments()` for those tickets, or (b) fetch the full comment list via the dedicated REST comment endpoint for enrichment, so the idempotency fix applies to both paths.
evidence: Plan states fix is 'get_comments() returns only the first 50 of N comments (no --paginate)' — names only the fallback path
`get_comment_map()` at acli_graph.py:354 requests `fields: ['comment']` in a bulk search; Jira Cloud caps inline comment sub-pages per issue in search responses

`_diff_comments` at outbound_comments.py:233-240: when `"comment"` key is present in snapshot (enrichment path), it reads `comment_field.get('comments', [])` directly and never calls `client.get_comments()` — the `--paginate` fix is entirely bypassed for enriched tickets

The plan's Success Criterion P1 requires '0 comment adds for a ticket already mirrored, even when the Jira side has >50 comments' — this test would still fail for enriched-path tickets if only the fallback is fixed
findings.finding: The P1 fix (adding `--paginate` to `get_comments()`) only corrects the per-ticket ACLI fallback path. The primary enrichment path — `get_comment_map()` using `POST /rest/api/3/search/jql` — returns inline `comment` fields for each issue with Jira Cloud's default sub-pagination cap (typically 5–10 comments per issue in a search response). For the 13 tickets near Jira's 5000-comment cap, `get_comment_map()` populates `snapshot[key]["comment"]["comments"]` with a truncated list. Since `_diff_comments` uses the snapshot-carried path exclusively when the `"comment"` key is present (bypassing `get_comments()` entirely), those tickets still produce false 'add' mutations for the unseen tail — the enrichment path suffers the same truncation defect via a different mechanism that the plan does not address.
findings.location: Plan §P1 / `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_graph.py` `get_comment_map()` + `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/outbound_comments.py` `_diff_comments()`
scenarios: Any ticket enriched by `get_comment_map()` with >N inline comments (N = Jira Cloud's search-inline cap, likely 5-10) would have a truncated `comment_field` in the snapshot, causing `_diff_comments` to emit add-mutations for the unseen comments every pass — the 'applied 0 of 0' idempotency criterion still fails

After the `--paginate` fix lands, tickets that fall back to `get_comments()` (those the search omits) will be fixed; tickets enriched by `get_comment_map()` with large comment counts will remain broken
findings.suggested_fix: In `get_comment_map()`, after extracting `comment_field`, check `comment_field.get('total', 0) > len(comment_field.get('comments', []))`: if true, omit the key from the enrichment result so the differ falls back to the per-ticket `get_comments()` (which the plan fixes with `--paginate`). This makes the "never-emit-blind" invariant also handle the sub-pagination case and requires no new API surface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the P1 fix mechanism against Jira Cloud's search API behavior: confirm whether `POST /rest/api/3/search/jql` with `fields=["comment"]` returns ALL comments per issue (not a truncated subset), and/or confirm that `jira workitem comment list --paginate` is a supported flag — document the verified path in the plan before implementation.
evidence: Plan: '`get_comments()` returns only the first 50 of N comments (no `--paginate`)'
`get_comment_map` in `acli_graph.py` requests `fields=["comment"]` from the Jira search API and stores the result verbatim; Jira Cloud search is known to truncate embedded comment arrays per issue
`get_comments` in `acli.py` and `acli_cli_ops.py` issues `jira workitem comment list --key KEY --json` with no `--paginate` flag; `--paginate` is present only on `workitem search` in the codebase
ABSENCE: plan does not address whether `get_comment_map`'s search-carried comment field is complete for 22–76 comment issues, nor whether `--paginate` is available on `comment list`
findings.finding: The plan asserts that P1 (comment sync idempotency for tickets with >50 comments) is fixable, but does not verify the capability of the chosen fix path. The `get_comment_map` implementation stores the raw Jira search `comment` field verbatim, yet Jira Cloud's `POST /rest/api/3/search/jql` is documented to truncate the embedded `comment` sub-object per issue (typically to 5 entries). If the bulk-search path is the intended fix, it may also return an incomplete comment list for the 13 capped tickets (22–76 comments each), leaving the idempotency criterion unmet. Separately, if the fix instead adds `--paginate` to the ACLI `jira workitem comment list` command, the plan does not verify that this flag is supported on that subcommand (it is documented for `workitem search` but not `comment list`).
findings.location: Context section (P1 description) / Success Criteria P1 / `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_graph.py` `get_comment_map` / `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli.py` `get_comments`
scenarios: Deploying P1 fix via `get_comment_map` bulk enrichment path: tickets with >5 comments still get a truncated comment list from search, dedup still misses comments, and the idempotency criterion is not met.
Deploying P1 fix via `--paginate` on `jira workitem comment list`: if the flag is unsupported, the ACLI command fails at runtime and the per-ticket fallback fails entirely.

The 13 capped tickets have 22–76 comments; even a correct per-ticket fallback via REST `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment` with proper pagination would work, but neither the plan nor the code confirms this path is used.
findings.suggested_fix: Use the Jira REST per-issue comment endpoint (`GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=100&startAt=N`) with explicit pagination for the dedup comparison on tickets where `comment.total > comment.comments.length` in the search result. This is a well-established Jira API pattern. Alternatively, confirm via a live probe that `jira workitem comment list --json` already returns all comments (no truncation) and document that finding.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `POST /rest/api/3/search/jql` with `fields=["comment"]` returns all comments per issue or a truncated first page; if truncated, specify how `get_comment_map()` or the per-ticket fallback handles issues where `comment.total > comment.maxResults`.
evidence: get_comment_map() reads `fields.get('comment')` verbatim from the search response — no check of `comment.total` vs `comment.maxResults`
acli_graph.py lines 398-403: the comment field is stored as-is; no secondary pagination for per-issue comments

The Jira REST search API (`/rest/api/3/search/jql`) returns the `comment` field truncated to a default page size per issue — separate per-issue pagination (`GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comments?startAt=N`) is required for full comment lists
Evidence class: Partially-verified — the search endpoint is live-proven, but the per-issue comment truncation behavior within search results is unconfirmed against the specific Jira instance
findings.finding: The plan's P1 fix (and the existing get_comment_map enrichment path) relies on `POST /rest/api/3/search/jql` with `fields=["comment"]` to fetch comment state for all tickets in one pass. However, Jira Cloud's REST search API truncates the inline `comment` field to a per-issue page (typically the first 5–10 comments; `comment.total` may exceed `comment.maxResults`). For the 13 tickets already at Jira's 5,000-comment cap, the enrichment path would return only a partial comment list, leaving the majority of duplicates invisible to the differ. The plan does not address whether `get_comment_map()` handles per-ticket comment pagination within the search response.
findings.location: Context section, P1 bullet; Success Criteria P1: 'outbound comment sync is idempotent … even when the Jira side has >50 comments'
scenarios: A ticket with 5,000 comments: get_comment_map returns only the first ~5–10 comments; the differ sees 22–76 local comments not in that truncated set and re-emits adds, reproducing the exact overflow bug the plan intends to fix

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that get_comment_map's per-issue comment field is not capped at 50 by Jira's search API; if it is, either request per-issue pagination or fall back to per-ticket get_comments for issues where comment.total > comment.comments.length.
evidence: acli_graph.py:311-411: get_comment_map issues POST /rest/api/3/search/jql with fields=['comment']; it reads fields.get('comment') verbatim — whatever Jira returns, which is capped at 50 per issue in search results.

fetcher.py:486-490: snapshot[snap_key]['comment'] = comment_field — this is then used by _diff_comments as the snapshot-carried path (client NOT consulted), so the 50-comment truncation applies silently.
Parent SC: '0 comment adds for a ticket already mirrored, even when the Jira side has >50 comments' — but get_comment_map only sees the first 50 per issue from Jira search.
container pairing: parent + 1f3d-cb66-d3fc-4356
findings.finding: get_comment_map (the bulk fast-path used by the fetcher) returns the Jira search result's comment field verbatim. Jira's REST search caps the comment field at maxResults=50 per issue by default, meaning get_comment_map silently truncates comments beyond 50 for each issue — the same truncation bug as get_comments, but in the bulk path. The child's plan does not acknowledge or address this.
findings.location: child 1f3d-cb66-d3fc-4356
scenarios: A ticket with 120 Jira comments: get_comment_map populates snapshot with only 50 comments; _diff_comments takes the snapshot-carried path (client not called) and re-posts comments 51-120 every pass — identical to the original bug, but now via the bulk path.
findings.suggested_fix: In the fetcher enrichment loop (fetcher.py:488-490), after storing comment_field, check if comment_field.get('total', 0) > len(comment_field.get('comments', [])). If so, omit the key from the snapshot (do NOT set snapshot[snap_key]['comment']) so the differ falls back to the per-ticket get_comments path — where the pagination fix will then apply.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Encode the P1/P2 → observability deployment ordering as an explicit dependency in the child-ticket list or Acceptance Criteria (e.g., 'Observability fix is deployed only after P1 and P2 are live on main').
evidence: Success Criteria: 'reports failure (non-zero exit) rather than `converged`, once P1/P2 remove the standing noise'
Acceptance Criteria lists observability as a peer child ticket with no ordering guard

If observability is deployed before P1/P2 are live, every pass will immediately exit non-zero due to existing noise (~815 inbound link applies/pass), making the reconciler operationally broken until P1/P2 land
findings.finding: The Success Criteria state the observability fix applies 'once P1/P2 remove the standing noise,' implying a deployment ordering constraint, but this ordering is absent from the Acceptance Criteria and the child-ticket list, creating a cross-section contradiction where the AC treats all five child tickets as co-equal closeable items.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Observability' bullet ('once P1/P2 remove the standing noise'); Acceptance Criteria — 'All child tickets (P1, P2, observability, test-hardening, Jira-cleanup) are closed'
scenarios: An engineer closes P2 and observability tickets in parallel; observability fix merges first; production reconciler starts exiting non-zero on every pass, breaking downstream consumers before P1/P2 are deployed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria or child-ticket notes: '[ ] Observability fix (non-zero exit on sub-op failure) is merged to main AFTER P1 (comment pagination) and P2 (link direction) fixes are confirmed live, to avoid spurious failures during the transition window.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add --paginate (or startAt loop) to get_comments() in BOTH acli_cli_ops.py and acli.py, and add a unit test that stubs acli returning 3 pages and asserts get_comments returns all 120 comments.
evidence: Child AC1: 'a unit test builds a fake acli that returns 3 pages (e.g. 50+50+20=120) and asserts get_comments returns all 120 — fails on the current single-page code.'
acli_cli_ops.py:522-531: get_comments invokes 'acli jira workitem comment list --key <k> --json' with NO --paginate.
acli.py:655-665: AcliClient.get_comments similarly has no --paginate.
test_outbound_differ_live_comment_fetch.py: all tests use MagicMock().get_comments.return_value = [...] — the pagination mechanic of get_comments itself is never exercised.
No test file in tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ contains a fake acli with multi-page comment responses.
container pairing: parent + 1f3d-cb66-d3fc-4356
findings.finding: The child's own AC1 requires a unit test that builds a fake acli returning 3 pages (50+50+20=120 comments) and asserts get_comments() returns all 120 — yet no such test exists anywhere in the repo, and get_comments() in both acli_cli_ops.py and acli.py remains un-paginated (no --paginate flag added). The existing tests only mock client.get_comments() returning a pre-assembled list; they verify _diff_comments dedup logic but not that get_comments() itself fetches beyond page 1.
findings.location: child 1f3d-cb66-d3fc-4356
scenarios: A ticket with 120 Jira comments: the per-ticket fallback calls get_comments(), which still returns only 50 (oldest), so _diff_comments re-posts comments 51-120 every pass — the parent P1 SC is not satisfied.
get_comment_map bulk path degrades (HTTP 410 or transient fault): fallback to per-ticket get_comments still truncates at 50, silently.
findings.suggested_fix: In acli_cli_ops.py:get_comments and acli.py:AcliClient.get_comments, add '--paginate' to the cmd list (matching the pattern in acli.py:search_issues). Then add a unit test in tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ (e.g., test_get_comments_pagination.py) that patches _run_acli/_run to return 3 JSON pages and asserts the combined 120-item list is returned.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test that constructs a 75+ comment Jira snapshot (via client.get_comments returning 120 items) and asserts 0 adds when the matching local comment is at position 75.
evidence: Child AC2: 'unit test with a >50-comment Jira snapshot where the matching local comment is comment #75 — asserts empty mutation list; RED on current code.'

test_outbound_differ_live_comment_fetch.py: test (a) uses local_bodies=['Investigation note', 'Fix plan', 'Testing results'] (3 comments), test (b) uses 3 comments — no test constructs a 75-comment Jira state.
test_outbound_differ_comment_dedup.py: all snapshots use 0-3 comments.
container pairing: parent + 1f3d-cb66-d3fc-4356
findings.finding: Child AC2 requires a unit test with a >50-comment Jira snapshot where the matching local comment is comment #75 — asserting empty mutation list and failing RED on current code. No such test exists; the existing live-fetch tests use only small comment sets (2-3 comments) where the matching comment is always within page 1.
findings.location: child 1f3d-cb66-d3fc-4356
scenarios: The specific regression case (local comment #75 already in Jira but beyond page 1) is not covered by any test — a fix that returns only the first 50 from a mocked response would still pass the existing tests.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend test_outbound_differ_live_comment_fetch.py with a test case where client.get_comments returns 120 comments (items 0-119) and the local ticket has one comment matching item #75 — assert zero mutations emitted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the Jira cleanup script processes comment deletions in small batches (e.g., ≤100 deletes/ticket per run) with per-call throttling to respect Jira API rate limits.
evidence: Plan says only: 'executed AFTER P1 is deployed to main' — no batch size, no sleep/throttle, no API-rate-limit handling is described.
13 tickets × up to 5000 comments = up to 65,000 potential comment deletions in a single operation.
findings.finding: The Jira cleanup step (de-duplicating up to ~65,000 comments across 13 tickets) specifies no batching or throttling, risking Jira API rate-limit exhaustion and partial completion with no defined recovery.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Jira data cleanup: the 13 capped tickets are de-duplicated'
scenarios: Jira rate-limits the deletion calls mid-cleanup, leaving some tickets partially de-duplicated and the reconciler still unable to sync new comments to those tickets.
A bulk deletion hits a Jira cloud per-minute API quota, causing HTTP 429 errors that abort the script without a clear retry/resume path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require that the cleanup script is idempotent and resumable: it must skip tickets already below the cap/already de-duplicated, and re-running it must produce the same result as running it once.
evidence: No idempotency mechanism (e.g., checkpoint file, dry-run comparison before delete, skip-if-already-clean logic) is mentioned in the plan.
The plan treats the cleanup as a single step with no sub-step tracking.
findings.finding: The Jira cleanup step has no resumability guarantee — if it fails partway through (e.g., after de-duplicating 6 of 13 tickets), re-running it may double-delete or skip already-processed tickets, depending on implementation.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Jira data cleanup: the 13 capped tickets are de-duplicated'
scenarios: The cleanup script crashes after processing 6 tickets; on re-run it either re-processes those 6 (double-deleting valid unique comments if dedup logic is fragile) or has no way to resume from ticket 7.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the reconciler must be quiesced (or the affected tickets locked) during the cleanup run, or explicitly document why concurrent reconciler execution is safe given P1's idempotency guarantee.
evidence: Plan only ensures P1 (pagination fix) is deployed before cleanup — it does not specify whether the reconciler is paused/quiesced during cleanup.
With the reconciler running live during cleanup, comment counts change underneath the dedup script, potentially invalidating its dedup decisions mid-execution.
findings.finding: The plan does not address the dual-write window during cleanup: the reconciler may run concurrently with the cleanup script and add new (now-idempotent) comments while old duplicates are being deleted, potentially causing the dedup script to miscount or miss newly-added entries.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Jira data cleanup: the 13 capped tickets are de-duplicated'; context: 'executed AFTER P1 is deployed to main'
scenarios: Reconciler adds a new legitimate comment to a ticket while the cleanup script is enumerating duplicates on that same ticket; the script's snapshot is stale and it deletes a comment that was just written.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'same-kind gaps' in the test-hardening success criterion — e.g., 'pagination gaps in any other reconciler `get_*` call that lacks `--paginate`' and 'any other `_diff_links_*` branch with swapped inward/outward labels' — so the scope is bounded and the criterion is evaluable.
evidence: 'same-kind gaps' is not defined anywhere in the plan
An executor cannot determine when this criterion is satisfied — are pagination gaps in other endpoints in scope? Are other swapped-direction link types? The plan gives no guidance.
This is a classic scope-deferral placeholder masquerading as a criterion.
findings.finding: The test-hardening success criterion includes 'AND same-kind gaps' with no definition of what constitutes a same-kind gap, leaving the scope of additional test cases entirely to executor discretion and making the criterion unmeasurable.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Test hardening' bullet: 'unit + integration + E2E coverage added for all three failure modes AND same-kind gaps'
scenarios: An agent implements tests for the three named failure modes and stops — is the criterion met?
An agent over-indexes and tests every paginated endpoint in the reconciler — is that the intent?
A reviewer rejects the PR claiming 'same-kind gaps' weren't covered, but no one can say what they are.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'AND same-kind gaps' with a concrete enumeration, e.g.: 'AND any other reconciler `get_*` call missing `--paginate` pagination (audit scope: the 4 remaining get_* calls in jira_reconciler.py) AND any other `_diff_links_*` branch where inward/outward label mapping is untested.' If no other same-kind gaps exist after a brief audit, state that explicitly: 'same-kind gap audit found none; coverage limited to the three named failure modes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion that explicitly verifies the observability fix: e.g., 'A synthetic pass with an injected sub-op failure (or a documented manual test) exits non-zero and does NOT log `converged`; `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` (or its replacement) is confirmed enabled in production config.'
evidence: Success Criteria: 'a pass with any silent sub-op no-op reports failure (non-zero exit) rather than `converged`, once P1/P2 remove the standing noise'

Acceptance Criteria: only '`rebar reconcile --mode reconcile-check` shows 0 comment-cap failures and 0 inbound link cycle-rejections' and 'All child tickets closed' — neither verifies non-zero exit or changed log output
findings.finding: The 'Observability' deliverable described in Success Criteria (non-zero exit, non-'converged' reporting on sub-op failures) has no covering Acceptance Criterion — the AC only checks that 0 failures occur at runtime, not that the exit-code/log behavior itself changed.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Observability' bullet; Acceptance Criteria section
scenarios: The observability child ticket is closed without actually enabling/wiring the non-zero exit path; the AC passes because P1/P2 are clean and the runtime check shows 0 failures, leaving the observability fix unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '[ ] A reconciler pass that encounters any sub-op failure exits non-zero and logs a failure summary rather than `converged`; verified by an integration test or documented manual verification step.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and audit all consumers of the reconciler's exit code before enabling `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP`; document any CI/script/monitoring changes needed, or provide a staged rollout (e.g., enable in non-blocking/advisory mode first) as the expand-contract path.
evidence: 'a pass with failed sub-ops still prints `applied N of N` / exits 0 / logs `converged`' — current consumers rely on exit 0
'once P1/P2 remove the standing noise' — the plan enables the flag but does not enumerate dependent callers of the reconciler exit code
ABSENCE: no mention of surveying downstream consumers (CI jobs, alerting, orchestration) before flipping the exit-code behavior
findings.finding: Enabling `RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP` changes the reconciler's exit code from 0 to non-zero when sub-operations fail. This is a breaking behavioral change for any CI pipelines, scripts, or monitoring integrations that currently consume the reconciler's exit 0 as 'healthy'. The plan does not acknowledge this breakage or provide a migration path.
findings.location: Context — 'Observability (meta)' / 'The RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP flag (default off) exists but is not enabled'
scenarios: A CI pipeline that calls `rebar reconcile` and checks `$?` == 0 to proceed to the next stage begins failing after the flag is enabled, even on legitimate transient Jira API errors
Monitoring that pages on non-zero exit codes generates false alerts during the transition period when P1/P2 noise is not yet fully zero

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and document all callers/consumers of the reconciler's exit code (CI, cron, monitoring) and add a migration note or flag-gated rollout plan for the non-zero exit behavior change.
evidence: 'a pass with any silent sub-op no-op reports failure (non-zero exit) rather than converged'
'RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP flag (default off) exists but is not enabled' — implies existing callers have been written against exit-0 semantics
Plan names no migration path, flag-rollout strategy, or caller audit for this behavioral contract change
findings.finding: The observability fix changes the reconciler's exit code from 0 to non-zero on sub-op failures, but the plan does not acknowledge downstream callers (CI pipelines, monitoring integrations, cron jobs, alerting rules) that depend on the current exit-0 behavior.
findings.location: Success Criteria — Observability bullet; Context — 'RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP flag (default off)'
scenarios: CI jobs that run the reconciler and treat non-zero exit as a build failure start alerting on every run until P1/P2 noise is cleared
Monitoring dashboards counting reconciler exits break their baseline
Automated rollback triggers fire unexpectedly if the reconciler is wired into deployment pipelines
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection under the Observability item listing known callers, confirm the RECONCILER_FAIL_SILENT_NOOP flag will gate the new behavior during rollout (default off → on after P1/P2 land and noise is zero), and specify when the flag is promoted to default-on.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify via `acli jira workitem comment list --help` (or an equivalent probe log) that `--paginate` is a supported flag on that specific subcommand; if absent, document the alternative (e.g., REST `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comments?startAt=N` pagination loop) before locking in the P1 fix approach.
evidence: acli.py get_comments() uses: `jira workitem comment list --key {key} --json` — no --paginate present
search_issues() uses `--paginate` flag on `jira workitem search`, but no evidence this flag exists on `jira workitem comment list`
No --help output, probe log, or ACLI docs reference in the repo confirms `--paginate` is valid for the `comment list` subcommand
Evidence class: Unverified — the specific flag surface on `jira workitem comment list` has not been probed
findings.finding: The plan's P1 root-cause diagnosis asserts that adding `--paginate` to the `get_comments()` ACLI call (`jira workitem comment list --key {key} --json`) will fix the 50-comment truncation, but no --help output, probe log, or docs reference in the repo confirms that `jira workitem comment list` actually supports the `--paginate` flag. The flag is verified only for `jira workitem search`. If `comment list` does not support `--paginate`, the stated fix approach has a capability gap that would require a different solution (e.g., REST-based pagination via `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comments?startAt=N`).
findings.location: Context section, P1 bullet: 'get_comments() returns only the first 50 of N comments (no --paginate)'
scenarios: P1 child ticket author adds `--paginate` to get_comments(), ships it, and the ACLI subprocess fails or silently ignores the flag — the 50-comment truncation persists and the fix appears to regress
findings.suggested_fix: Run `acli jira workitem comment list --help` and record the output in the plan or a linked probe log. If `--paginate` is unsupported, replace the fix approach with a REST pagination loop: `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comments?startAt=0&maxResults=100`, incrementing `startAt` until `startAt + maxResults >= total`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC2 verification: state that `--mode reconcile-check` is the primary in-session evidence and that a live pass log is supplementary (or required only if CI lacks Jira credentials), so the executor knows which artifact closes the ticket.
evidence: '(or a live pass)' provides no guidance on when the alternative applies
CI environments may not have live Jira credentials; an executor needs to know which evidence satisfies the AC
This is a minor defaultable gap — the reconcile-check mode is already named first, implying preference, but the plan should state the default.
findings.finding: AC2 offers 'rebar reconcile --mode reconcile-check (or a live pass)' without specifying which evidence is preferred or under what conditions the alternative is acceptable, leaving the verification path ambiguous for an executor who cannot run a live Jira reconciler pass in CI.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — second bullet: '`rebar reconcile --mode reconcile-check` (or a live pass) shows 0 comment-cap failures and 0 inbound link cycle-rejections'
scenarios: CI passes with reconcile-check but a reviewer demands a live-pass log before closing — undefined which is authoritative
An executor without live Jira access assumes reconcile-check suffices; a different reviewer rejects it.
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite as: 'Primary: `rebar reconcile --mode reconcile-check` against the staging Jira instance shows 0 comment-cap failures and 0 inbound-link cycle-rejections for the 13 previously-capped tickets and the ~815 formerly-affected link tickets. If staging credentials are unavailable in CI, a live-pass log attached to the ticket satisfies this AC.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion that verifies the test-hardening deliverable: e.g., 'CI test suite includes named unit/integration/E2E tests for comment pagination, link-direction swap, and observability; all pass on post-fix code and fail on pre-fix code (confirmed via CI run or diff).'
evidence: Success Criteria: 'unit + integration + E2E coverage added for all three failure modes AND same-kind gaps; the new tests fail on the pre-fix code'

Acceptance Criteria: '`rebar reconcile --mode reconcile-check` (or a live pass) shows 0 comment-cap failures and 0 inbound link cycle-rejections' — no AC item verifies test suite presence or green/red status
findings.finding: The 'Test hardening' deliverable described in Success Criteria has no covering Acceptance Criterion — the AC only verifies runtime reconcile-check output, not the existence or pass/fail status of the added unit/integration/E2E tests.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Test hardening' bullet; Acceptance Criteria section
scenarios: An implementer ships P1/P2 fixes without any new tests; the AC is satisfied (runtime passes), but the test-hardening deliverable silently goes undelivered.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '[ ] CI reports green for the new comment-pagination, link-direction, and observability tests on main; a revert of each fix causes the corresponding test(s) to fail.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the P1 section explaining why the chosen fix path (ACLI `--paginate` on the fallback, plus any enrichment-path truncation guard) is the appropriate approach — e.g. 'the batch enrichment path is kept as primary for fleet-wide latency; the fallback is fixed for correctness at high comment counts; truncation detection bridges the gap'.
evidence: Plan describes the defect and the fix ('no --paginate') but contains no statement of why the enrichment-map approach (batch search) is preferred over per-ticket comment fetch for correctness at scale
ABSENCE: no 'approach rationale' or 'why this fix' section anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for why the batch REST `get_comment_map()` enrichment approach is the right mechanism for delivering full comment state versus, for example, a per-ticket REST comment endpoint call that can paginate independently. The plan implicitly inherits the existing design without justifying it as the right fit for the >50-comment case specifically.
findings.location: Plan §Context / §P1 approach — no rationale section
scenarios: An implementer choosing between fixing the ACLI fallback vs. fixing the enrichment path vs. switching to the REST per-ticket comment API has no authored guidance from the plan on the intended design
